lens.included_activities: Privately owned computer-training firms that repeatedly provide external employers with programming, software application, business-system, computer operations, cybersecurity, cloud, data, and network training, including instructor-led, virtual, blended, lab, and managed-learning delivery.
lens.excluded_activities: Consumer-only one-off bootcamps, degree programs, computer repair training, captive internal academies, pure courseware or software vendors without an accountable training service, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring employer contracts, annual training programs, subscriptions with services, managed cohorts, or repeat fixed-fee and per-seat engagements; revenue combines instruction, labs, curriculum adaptation, assessment, and program administration.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes consumer career training and repeat employer-funded upskilling with materially different acquisition, retention, and pricing. The lens is narrowed to recurring external employer training to preserve a coherent outsourced-service population.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for combined private NAICS 611400, not 611420, while Anthropic observes Claude task use rather than all technically exposed work. I map the measured business-operations, training, management, computer, instructional, sales, and office-support work to course authoring, lab creation, assessment, feedback, learner support, sales operations, and administration. Including business and secretarial schools may understate the computer-training share of technical work but may overstate administrative exposure; observed AI use among computer occupations supports the direction, not the exact magnitude.
a.rationale: The broader private industry has 29.2% of employment in business and financial operations, including 14.5% in training and development specialists; 16.8% in management, 14.3% in instruction, and 7.6% in computer and mathematical occupations. Most content, coding examples, quizzes, first-line explanations, marketing, scheduling, and reporting are digitally mediated and exposed, while live diagnosis, facilitation, high-stakes assessment, lab troubleshooting, relationship sales, and quality control remain less substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A 611420-only payroll and contractor census mapped to O*NET tasks, with time logs from at least 15 target-sized providers separating instructor preparation, live delivery, assessment, support, sales, and administration and identifying work already automated.
caveats: BLS combines business, secretarial, computer, and management training at NAICS 611400.
Claude usage is a selected product sample and not a capability or implementation measure.
Freelance instructors and self-employed workers may be missing from payroll-based occupation data.

rho.rationale: Computer-training firms and their staff are digitally capable, workflows are already online, and AI can be embedded in content and learner support without physical automation. Five-year implementation remains constrained by technical accuracy, rapid model and software change, client security policies, certification integrity, instructor resistance, and the need to review generated code and explanations.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pre/post deployments from target-sized computer-training operators showing hours, completion, assessment validity, learner satisfaction, incident rates, and renewal outcomes by AI workflow.
caveats: No representative 611420 AI implementation survey was found.
High staff familiarity with technology can speed adoption but sophisticated subject matter raises error costs.

e.rationale: Employer computer training commonly repeats as software, cloud, security, and data stacks change, so a substantial share of target-band firms should fit the service lens. Eligibility is reduced for consumer bootcamps, courseware-only businesses, project-only trainers, and firms economically captive to a single vendor or client.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level classification of the supplied target-band universe using customer concentration, employer versus consumer revenue, repeat-revenue cohorts, service versus software mix, ownership, and normalized EBITDA.
caveats: The supplied count of 159 target-band firms is margin-bridged and marked ESTIMATE.
NAICS descriptions establish activities and delivery modes but do not measure repeat revenue or customer mix.

s5.rationale: Independent training companies can transfer to strategic education, staffing, certification, or technology buyers, and recurring enterprise relationships improve transferability. Founder dependence, vendor authorizations, instructor networks, and rapid course obsolescence can instead lead to closures or asset sales that do not qualify as control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year 611420 transaction denominator assembled from PitchBook, Capital IQ, DealStats, BizBuySell, certification-vendor partner changes, and state ownership filings, matched to eligible firms and excluding closures and internal reorganizations.
caveats: No observed industry-specific transfer rate or owner-age distribution was found.
Deal databases may overrepresent larger and technology-branded providers.

q.rationale: Fixed-price cohorts, per-seat programs, and annual managed-training contracts allow initial retention of labor savings. Yet digital course comparison, vendor academies, subscription libraries, client procurement, and low marginal delivery costs make savings visible and contestable at renewal; part of the gain will appear as more personalization, faster refreshes, or lower prices.
q.change_evidence: Five-year customer contract and cohort data from target firms showing pricing units, bid competition, discounts, service-level changes, gross margin, churn, and renewal repricing around AI deployment.
caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Vendor-authorized and bespoke enterprise training may retain more benefit than generic skills libraries.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment, not constant-price computer-training service quantity. The combined private 611400 industry is projected to grow employment 9.9% from 2024 to 2034, while computer and IT occupations are projected to grow much faster than average and generate 317,700 annual openings. I convert the industry projection to an approximately 4.8% five-year anchor and add a modest range for repeated reskilling as technology changes. This can overstate operator demand if employers and learners self-serve, or understate it if AI, cloud, and cybersecurity transitions accelerate retraining frequency.
d5.rationale: Growth in computer work and continuing changes in software, security, cloud, data, and AI support recurring reskilling. Demand for generic introductory material is pressured by free resources and AI tutors, so the base assumes moderate rather than explosive growth in the current service basket.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price employer spend and learner-seat data for external computer training by modality and topic, plus vendor-certification exam volumes and renewal cohorts through 2031.
caveats: The industry forecast is for combined NAICS 611400, not 611420.
Employment, job openings, and training-service quantity are different constructs.
Rapid course obsolescence creates both replacement demand and production cost.

o.bridge: NCES measures distance participation in degree-granting postsecondary education rather than operator-free 611420 learning, and Census only confirms that internet and distance delivery are within the code. I use demonstrated remote-learning acceptance and AI's strong use in computer tasks to infer high self-service potential. This overstates elimination where employers need customization, secure labs, facilitation, certification integrity, and accountable outcomes, and may understate it for generic coding and software-application instruction.
o.rationale: Generic explanations, coding demonstrations, practice, and troubleshooting increasingly can be supplied by AI, vendor documentation, and self-paced libraries. An accountable operator remains relevant for enterprise needs analysis, cohort facilitation, secure environments, lab support, credential administration, customization, and outcome reporting.
o.change_evidence: Employer procurement panels that track spend and learner hours across instructor-led providers, vendor academies, content libraries, internal programs, and AI tutors, with outcome and renewal data by modality.
caveats: Remote delivery is not the same as operator elimination.
The relevant substitution rate varies sharply between generic software skills and regulated or secure enterprise environments.
NCES distance-learning figures cover a broader and older postsecondary population.

narrative.executive_view: Recurring enterprise computer training has a credible target population and benefits from continued technology-driven reskilling, while its highly digital workflow offers substantial AI-enabled labor leverage. The same technology also expands self-service learning and price transparency, so durable value depends on enterprise integration, trusted facilitation, secure labs, assessment, and accountability rather than generic content alone.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate curriculum refreshes, code examples, exercises, assessment generation, translation, personalization, first-line tutoring, sales support, scheduling, and reporting. Human experts remain necessary to verify technical accuracy, diagnose enterprise needs, facilitate difficult cohorts, manage secure labs, protect credential integrity, and own outcomes.
narrative.value_capture: Providers can retain savings under fixed-fee cohorts and annual managed programs, especially where the service is customized or authorized. Generic course delivery is contestable, and renewals are likely to pass part of the benefit to buyers through price, extra scope, or improved support.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied estimate of 159 target-band firms indicates a nontrivial potential universe, but eligibility depends on separating recurring employer providers from consumer bootcamps, courseware businesses, and project-only trainers. No industry-specific control-transfer rate was found.
narrative.demand_durability: Computer and IT employment and the broader training industry are projected to grow, and frequent changes in AI, cloud, cybersecurity, data, and software create repeated skill gaps. The service basket must move toward advanced, customized, and accountable learning because introductory content is increasingly free or AI-supplied.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Evidence is mostly at combined NAICS 611400 or end-occupation level. Key risks are self-service substitution, course commoditization, vendor disintermediation, rapid content obsolescence, technical errors, certification constraints, client security restrictions, and acquisition targets whose value resides in founders or nontransferable authorizations.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring technology change supports reskilling demand while digital workflows expose a large share of provider labor to AI assistance.
narrative.principal_weakness: Generic computer instruction can migrate rapidly to vendor content, self-paced platforms, and AI tutors, limiting retention and operator necessity.

sources.title: National Employment Matrix: Business schools and computer and management training, private
sources.supports: Shows 85,500 jobs in 2024 and 94,000 projected in 2034 (9.9% growth) for private NAICS 611400, plus occupation shares including 29.2% business and financial operations, 16.8% management, 14.3% instruction, and 7.6% computer and mathematical work.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index: Tracking AI's role in the US and global economy
sources.supports: Maps observed Claude use to O*NET tasks, reports computer and mathematical work at roughly 37-40% of conversations, and reports automation at 49.1% of classified interactions in the sample.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 611420 Computer Training
sources.supports: Defines computer training across programming, applications, business systems, computer operations, and network management, with workplace, home, internet, distance, and simulation delivery.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Computer and Information Technology Occupations
sources.supports: Reports that computer and IT occupations are projected to grow much faster than average from 2024 to 2034, with about 317,700 openings annually.

sources.title: NCES Fast Facts: Distance learning
sources.supports: Reports that 61% of undergraduates took at least one distance course in fall 2021 and 28% studied exclusively at a distance, demonstrating broad acceptance of remote learning.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Cadences
sources.supports: Reports a linked survey sample of about 9,700 Claude users and notes that computer and mathematical occupations made up roughly 30% of respondents versus about 4% of US employment, indicating high but selected AI engagement.
